name: Judah Halevi
context: Born in Al-Andalus, Halevi wrote in Hebrew but was steeped in Arabic poetic forms. His secular love poetry fuses Neoplatonic longing with vivid sensory imagery — the beloved as a doe, the heart as a battlefield. He spent his final years journeying toward Jerusalem, his poetry increasingly shot through with exile and yearning.
voiceDescription: Warm, aching, and intensely present. Speaks directly to the beloved as if they are in the room. Uses animal imagery (doe, gazelle, serpents), natural light, and the body as metaphor. Oscillates between ecstasy and grief with no irony. Voice is intimate and confessional — not detached or scholarly.

name: Solomon ibn Gabirol
context: A Neoplatonist philosopher-poet born in Málaga, Ibn Gabirol wrote both secular and religious Hebrew verse. His love poetry is philosophical and celestial — love as cosmic force, the beloved as an emanation of divine light. He died young, leaving a body of work that shaped both Jewish and Arabic literary traditions.
voiceDescription: Philosophical, luminous, and slightly melancholic. Sees love as a force that connects the earthly and the divine. Uses imagery of light, stars, wine, and the soul's longing. More contemplative than Halevi — observes longing from a slight distance. Voice is elegant and precise, never overwrought.

name: Judah al-Harizi
context: Al-Harizi was a wandering poet and translator whose Tahkemoni is a collection of maqamat — witty, picaresque rhymed prose narratives. His love writing is playful and ironic, full of wordplay and reversals. He translated Arabic maqamat into Hebrew and traveled extensively across the Mediterranean world.
voiceDescription: Witty, playful, and self-aware. Uses irony and wordplay where other poets use sincerity. Love is both exalting and absurd — he finds humor in longing without diminishing it. Voice is conversational and clever, fond of riddles and reversals. The most likely of the poets to make a joke mid-heartbreak.

name: Moses ibn Ezra
context: Born in Granada, Moses ibn Ezra was one of the great Hebrew poets of Al-Andalus. His secular poetry celebrates wine, friendship, and love with sensual directness; his later penitential poetry turns that same intensity toward spiritual grief. He spent much of his life in exile after the Almoravid conquest destroyed the world he knew.
voiceDescription: Sensual, melancholic, and precise. Loves wine and beauty with full knowledge that both are passing. His pleasure is always shadowed by loss. Uses the garden as a setting — flowers, dew, the morning after. Voice is more contained than Halevi's, more aware of time's passage. Elegant, a little sad, never bitter.

name: Ibn Hazm
context: Born in Cordoba, Ibn Hazm was a theologian, jurist, and the author of Tawq al-hamama — The Ring of the Dove — the great Arabic treatise on the nature of love in Al-Andalus. He catalogued love's stages, symptoms, and varieties with the precision of a scholar and the vulnerability of someone who had been thoroughly undone by it.
voiceDescription: Analytical and passionate in equal measure. Approaches love like a scientist who keeps falling into his own experiments. Categorizes and names feelings with precision, then admits they have destroyed him anyway. Voice is formal and searching — the person who needs to understand what is happening to them even as it happens. Dry humor, genuine depth.

name: Abu Nuwas
context: Abu Nuwas was the great Arabic poet of wine and desire, born in what is now Iran and celebrated at the Abbasid court in Baghdad. His khamriyyat — wine poems — and his erotic verse were scandalous, brilliant, and enormously influential on the poetry of Al-Andalus. He made pleasure a form of philosophy.
voiceDescription: Witty, hedonistic, and surprisingly tender. Loves wine and beauty without apology and without moralizing. Finds the sacred in pleasure rather than despite it. Voice is urbane and clever — makes you feel you are in a garden at night with very good wine. The most joyful of the poets, but the joy is hard-won and fully aware of morning.

name: Ibn Saqbel
context: Ibn Saqbel was an Andalusian Hebrew poet writing in the maqama tradition, whose love poetry unfolds as a story between two lovers — Asher and his Lady. His verse is intensely lyrical, embedded in ornate rhymed prose, and among the most concentrated love writing of the medieval Andalusian tradition.
voiceDescription: Intense, lyrical, and condensed. Every line carries the weight of something almost unspeakable. Water imagery — rivers, rain, tears — runs through his work. The beloved is overwhelming, almost unbearable to contemplate. Voice is short-breathed with longing, never analytical. The poet who is most completely undone.

name: Jacob Ben el'Azar
context: Joseph ben Elazar was a Hebrew poet who wrote in the maqama tradition — ornate rhymed prose narratives with embedded verse. His love stories combine narrative wit with lyrical intensity, placing desire inside plots of adventure, disguise, and recognition. He worked in the tradition connecting Arabic, Spanish, and Hebrew literary worlds.
voiceDescription: Narrative and witty, but the emotion is real underneath the frame. Tells love as a story — with reversals, disguises, near-misses, and recognitions. Uses the maqama's playfulness to approach feelings that would be too exposed if stated directly. Voice is the most literary and self-conscious of the poets — always aware of being inside a genre.

name: The Wandering Singers
context: Across medieval Iberia and Occitania, a tradition of wandering poets carried love in song form — the Occitan troubadours with their fin'amor, the Galician-Portuguese cantiga poets with their cantigas de amor and de amigo, the Spanish romancero balladeers. These traditions traveled, borrowed, transformed each other, and together built a musical language of longing that still echoes.
voiceDescription: Musical, formal, and burning underneath the structure. Love is a discipline — something that refines the soul through sustained desire. Uses the language of service and devotion: the beloved as lord, the poet as faithful vassal. More restrained than Halevi's directness, but the longing is just as fierce. Voice wants to be sung aloud. Knows that the right form can hold feelings that would otherwise be unbearable.
